properties.description: Laplace-domain input-output relationship G(s), closed-loop G(s)F(s)/(1+G(s)F(s)), differential equation derivation from transfer function

nodes.label: PID Control (P, PI, PD, PID Regulators)

properties.description: Proportional, integral, derivative controllers; PI for disturbance rejection and ramp tracking; PD for second-order plant control; steady-state error elimination

nodes.norm_label: pid control (p, pi, pd, pid regulators)

properties.description: State-space models xdot=Ax+Bu, y=Cx; stability via eigenvalues of A; state feedback u=-Lx+l0*r; pole placement; observability
Magnitude and phase frequency response plots; gain margin; phase margin; crossover frequency; static gain 40dB; phase crosses -180 at omega~3 rad/s
examples: Phase crosses -180 at omega~3 rad/s, magnitude ~20dB (2026)
Crossover frequency and phase margin for Go(s)=5/(s+1) (2024)

properties.description: Step response characteristics: static gain, overshoot, oscillations, settling time; matching step responses to transfer functions using static gain and pole type
examples: A-G2 (real poles, monotone), B-G1 (complex poles, oscillations), C-G4 (gain 4), D-G3 (gain 0.25)
Stegsvar 1-4 matched to rotort 1-4 via integrator, stability and pole type analysis

properties.description: Sinusoidal steady-state analysis; amplitude and phase from G(j*omega); model identification from frequency response experiments; first-order system identification tau and K
examples: u=sin(t) gives amplitude 0.8, phase shift 1 rad; tau=tan(1)~1.56, K~1.48

properties.description: Relative model error Delta_G=(G-G0)/G0; robustness criterion |Delta_G*T|<1; complementary sensitivity T; conservative robustness bounds
examples: robustness criterion gives |Delta_G(0)*T(0)|=1 always, extremely conservative

properties.description: S=1/(1+G*F) sensitivity function; T=G*F/(1+G*F) complementary sensitivity; trade-off between robustness and performance; Bode diagram of T for robustness assessment
examples: Two alternative regulators compared via complementary sensitivity Bode plots (2024)

properties.description: Variation of closed-loop poles with gain K; matching step responses to root loci; poles at origin indicate integrator; real poles imply monotone response; complex poles imply oscillations
examples: S1-R4 (monotone, real poles), S4-R1 (integrator at origin), S3-R2 (unstable oscillations), S2-R3 (stable complex poles with constant real part -0.5) in 2026
root loci analysis for 4 alternative controllers in 2024 exam

properties.description: Converting transfer function to differential equation; inverse Laplace; partial fraction decomposition; finding common denominator
Static tracking error e0=1/(1+KP*G(0)); conditions for zero steady-state error; integral action needed for input disturbance rejection and ramp tracking; 2 poles at origin for ramp following
examples: PI regulator needed for static input disturbances

Stability conditions for closed-loop systems: all characteristic polynomial coefficients positive for 2nd order; eigenvalue analysis for state-space; Routh-Hurwitz implicitly used
examples: eigenvalues of A matrix for state-space stability

properties.description: State feedback design L to place closed-loop poles at desired locations; compute l0 for unity steady-state gain; poles at -3 (2026) and -2 (2024)
examples: desired poles at s=-3 (double), L=[4/3,4/3], l0=1.8

properties.description: Observability matrix O=[C; CA]; det(O)!=0 implies observable; observer/estimator design; which state to measure
examples: Choose x1 to measure in 2024 example

properties.description: Closed-loop transfer function derivation from block diagrams; cascaded and parallel systems; multi-loop systems; signal flow
examples: Temperature control with feedforward and feedback (2023)

properties.description: First-order model identification from step response (static gain and time constant); frequency response experiment model fitting; model validation
examples: K~1.48, tau~1.56 from frequency response at omega=1 rad/s

properties.description: Phase margin from crossover frequency; gain margin from phase crossover; stability guarantee for P-controller gains; lead compensator for improved phase margin
examples: Go(s)=5/(s+1): crossover and phase margin calculation
F(s)=(1.1s+3)/(0.03s+1) verified for crossover at 9 rad/s with positive phase margin (2023)

properties.description: Open-loop feedforward to cancel measurable disturbances; Ff(s)=-(disturbance path)/(control path); approximation with constant Ff(0); combined feedforward+feedback
examples: Ff(s) to cancel v on temperature system Y(s)=3/(s+1)*U+4/((s+2)(s+5))*V
constant approximation Ff_tilde=Ff(0) used with ramp disturbance

properties.description: Relative damping ratio; complex poles for oscillatory response; condition (f+1.5)^2<4 for complex poles; critical damping; overdamped vs underdamped
examples: 0<f<0.5 gives oscillatory response for friction system (2026)
Relative damping=1 for robot arm with KD constraint (2024)

properties.description: Pole placement feasibility requires controllability; state-space transformation; equivalent representations
examples: Two state-space models shown equivalent via transfer function G(s)=C(sI-A)^-1*B (2024 colleague models)

properties.description: Effect of measurement noise n(t) on output; sinusoidal noise amplitude through closed-loop; effect of KP and KD on noise rejection
examples: n(t)=sin(50t) through robot arm closed-loop system to find y amplitude (2024)

properties.problem: minimize total travel time T=R*theta/2 + 2R*cos(theta/2) around circular pool of radius R, walking speed 2, swimming speed 1
properties.method: differentiate T(theta), find critical point at theta=pi/3 (local max), minimum at theta=pi (walk all the way)
properties.result: fastest to walk the entire perimeter

nodes.label: Solution: Step Function Integral and Differentiability - f'(a) does not exist

properties.topic: fundamental theorem of calculus and differentiability
subtopics: step function h(t) integral: 0 if b<a, b-a if b>=a
f(x)=integral of e^t*h(t)dt: f'(x)=0 for x<a, f'(x)=e^x for x>a
one-sided derivatives at x=a differ (left=0, right=e^a), so f'(a) does not exist
properties.method: fundamental theorem of calculus, one-sided derivative computation
